title: Transfer Learning
learning_outcomes: Distinguish between feature extraction and fine-tuning paradigms, defining the conditions that determine which approach to use.
Formulate the mathematical representation of parameter freezing and trace gradient paths in frozen networks.
Design adaptation strategies for custom heads, including learning rate scaling and catastrophic forgetting mitigation.

content_sections.title: Paradigms of Transfer Learning: Feature Extraction vs. Fine-Tuning
content_sections.content:
Transfer learning leverages knowledge learned from a source domain (e.g. ImageNet) to perform tasks in a target domain. This is achieved using two primary training paradigms:

### 1. Feature Extraction (Backbone Freezing)
In feature extraction, the pre-trained neural network (the 'backbone') is treated as a fixed feature extractor. 
*   The original task-specific classification head is removed and replaced with a custom head aligned with the target domain classes.
*   The weights of the backbone $\mathbf{W}_{\text{backbone}}$ are frozen, meaning they are not updated during optimization. Only the parameters of the new classification head $\mathbf{W}_{\text{head}}$ are trained.
*   **Applicability:** Best suited when the target dataset is small and highly similar to the source dataset, preventing overfitting.

### 2. Fine-Tuning (Full or Partial Network Training)
In fine-tuning, the custom classification head is added, but the backbone weights are updated during training.
*   **Full Fine-Tuning:** Backpropagation updates all weights across the entire network (both backbone and head).
*   **Partial Fine-Tuning:** The lower layers (capturing general features like edges and textures) are frozen, while the higher layers (capturing domain-specific shapes) and the classification head are trained.
*   **Applicability:** Best suited when the target dataset is large, or when it is small but highly dissimilar to the source dataset.

callouts.title: Freezing Saves Forward Pass Compute
callouts.content: A common mistake is thinking that freezing layers saves forward pass compute. The forward pass math is identical for frozen and trainable layers; every layer must still compute activations. Freezing only reduces backward pass compute by skipping gradient calculations and parameter updates.

content_sections.title: Layer Freezing Mechanics & Gradient Flows
content_sections.content:
Freezing is mathematically represented by isolating parameter subsets during backpropagation.

### 1. Gradient Zeroing
During optimization, the parameters of the network are updated using gradient descent:

$$\\mathbf{W}_l^{(t+1)} = \\mathbf{W}_l^{(t)} - \\eta \\frac{\\partial J}{\\partial \\mathbf{W}_l}$$

If a layer $l$ is frozen, we enforce:

$$\\frac{\\partial J}{\\partial \\mathbf{W}_l} = \\mathbf{0}$$

This ensures $\\mathbf{W}_l^{(t+1)} = \\mathbf{W}_l^{(t)}$, locking the weights in place.

### 2. Skipping Backpropagation Calculations
In deep learning frameworks, setting $\\text{requires\\_grad} = \\text{False}$ on parameters does more than just zero the weight updates. It intercepts the chain rule calculations. If all layers up to layer $l$ are frozen, the framework does not need to compute gradients of the loss with respect to the activations of those layers. This reduces backpropagation memory and compute requirements.

callouts.title: Batch Normalization Gotcha
callouts.content: When freezing a convolutional backbone, you must set Batch Normalization layers to evaluation mode (e.g. using `model.eval()` or freezing BN statistics). If left in training mode, the running stats (mean and variance) will continue to update using the target dataset's mini-batches, which can distort the pre-trained feature scales.

content_sections.title: Custom Head Adaptation & Learning Rate Scaling
content_sections.content:
Integrating a pre-trained backbone with a new classification head requires balancing feature preservation with adaptation.

### 1. Catastrophic Forgetting
If you fine-tune a pre-trained network using a large learning rate, the incoming gradients from the randomly initialized head can disrupt the pre-trained backbone weights. This degradation of pre-trained knowledge is known as **catastrophic forgetting**.

### 2. Differential Learning Rates
To prevent catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning, we apply different learning rates (learning rate scaling) to different layers. We allocate a large learning rate for the new custom head (which must learn from scratch) and a small learning rate for the pre-trained backbone:

$$\eta_{\text{backbone}} \approx 0.1 \times \eta_{\text{head}}$$

### 3. Stepwise Fine-Tuning Protocol
A standard industry best practice is a two-stage training protocol:
*   **Stage 1:** Freeze the backbone entirely and train the custom head with a standard learning rate (e.g. $\eta = 10^{-3}$) until it converges. This pre-aligns the head weights with the backbone's features.
*   **Stage 2:** Unfreeze the backbone (or its higher layers) and run fine-tuning on the entire network using a small learning rate (e.g. $\eta = 10^{-5}$) to adapt the features without disrupting them.
